title: 📝 Fix topic repository list not being displayed and `skip_users` not being applied
body:
## Description

We have 2 regressions in docs after we switched to Zensical:
* topic repository list is not displayed: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/external-links/#github-repositories
* lists of users in FastAPI People are not being filtered with `skip_users` list, so we can see Dependabot, GitHub actions in top contributors: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/fastapi-people/#top-contributors

The reason is that `macros` plugin doesn't support files with top level lists.
To resolve the issue we can convert data files to be mappings

## AI Disclaimer

Used AI for investigating things

## Checklist

- [ ] This PR links to a GitHub Discussion for the proposed code change.
- [ ] I added tests for the change.
- [ ] The new or updated tests fail on the main branch and pass on this PR.
- [ ] Coverage stays at 100%.
- [ ] The documentation explains the change if needed.

Checked by running `scripts/topic_repos.py` script locally that it produces correct data file
author: YuriiMotov

body: Bump pre-commit hook versions via `prek auto-update --freeze --cooldown-days 7`.
author: tiangolo

title: 👷 Use `FASTAPI_LATEST_CHANGES` token in `bump-pre-commit-hooks` workflow
body: Use `FASTAPI_LATEST_CHANGES` token in `bump-pre-commit-hooks` workflow as in other repositories.
author: YuriiMotov

title: 👷 Add GH workflow to bump pre-commit hook versions
body:
Dependabot doesn't work well with "pre-commit" package ecosystem..

This PR disables Dependabot "pre-commit" package ecosystem and adds a GH workflow to bump pre-commit hooks using `prek auto-update --freeze --cooldown-days 7` command instead.

---

Tested on my test repo - it works
author: YuriiMotov

title: 🔧 Set Dependabot schedule interval to "monthly"
body:
Weekly dependency updates are too noisy and not needed.

Don't update interval for `pre-commit` ecosystem as we decided to remove it.
author: YuriiMotov

title: 👷 Fix notify translations checkout target
body:
## What changed

- Configure the `notify-translations` workflow checkout step to use the repository default branch explicitly.
- This keeps `pull_request_target` runs on trusted base-repository code when `actions/checkout@v7` is used.

## Why

`actions/checkout@v7` refuses fork PR code in trusted `pull_request_target` contexts. The workflow only needs trusted repository code to run the notification script/action.

## Validation

- Ran `git diff --check` for the changed workflow.

## AI Disclaimer

Using Codex with GPT-5. Reviewed manually.
author: tiangolo

title: 👷 Fix latest-changes checkout target
body:
## What changed

- Configure the `latest-changes` workflow checkout step to use the repository default branch explicitly.
- This keeps `pull_request_target` runs on trusted base-repository code when `actions/checkout@v7` is used.

## Why

`actions/checkout@v7` refuses fork PR code in trusted `pull_request_target` contexts. For merged PR release-note workflows, the safe target is the base repository default branch.

## Validation

- Ran `git diff --check` for the changed workflow.
- Verified the pattern end to end in `tiangolo/sandbox`.

## AI Disclaimer

Using Codex with GPT-5. Reviewed manually.
author: tiangolo

title: ⬆️ Update issue-manager to 0.8.1
body:
## Description

Update the `tiangolo/issue-manager` GitHub Action pin to `0.8.1`.

This picks up the action runtime fix so the Docker action uses its baked-in virtual environment instead of letting `uv run` inspect the consumer repository Python version configuration.

## Checks

- `yq '.jobs' .github/workflows/issue-manager.yml`
- `git diff --check`

comments.author: YuriiMotov
comments.body:
Asked Claude Code to review and suggest initial list of specific term translations. Opened PR: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15810

Re-generated translated pages with the updated prompt.

Main thing is to see if there are any common terms or phrases that LLM translates incorretly or inconsistently, and we want to tell it to use specific translation for this term.
In translations for other languages we often tell it to use English terms in specific cases instead of translating them.
If you see such terms - we can add them to the list of special terms in language-specific LLM-prompt (see PR https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15810).

See how it's done for other languages:
* [Spanish](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/docs/es/llm-prompt.md?plain=1)
* [Russian](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/docs/ru/llm-prompt.md?plain=1)

Just leave comments with your suggestions here or in PR https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15810

comments.author: YuriiMotov
comments.body: @igennova, @ABcDexter, thanks for the reviews!

comments.author: YuriiMotov
comments.body: @igennova, could you please take a look at https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15810? It needs the approval from one more native speaker to be merged

title: 🐛 Fix `on_startup` and `on_shutdown` parameters of `APIRouter`
body:
This fixes the issue reported in this comment: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14851#issuecomment-3863896385

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI


def s_start():
    print("SYNC START", flush=True)


app = FastAPI(on_startup=[s_start])


@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"ok": True}
```

Startup event is not called because `APIRouter.on_startup` is set in `APIRouter.__init__` before calling `super().__init__` and  is overridden by `router.__init__` (https://github.com/Kludex/starlette/blob/e5b8a5d200504a2608bed11fe6f66b1d20b9ae2c/starlette/routing.py#L597-L598)
author: YuriiMotov

title: ✏️ Fix grammar, typos, and broken links in docs
body:
Fixed typos and grammar mistakes in docs.

I used Claude Code to find and fix mistakes, but carefully reviewed all corrections and asked to rephrase a couple of sentences to make them easier to read.

---

docs live command showed that there were some broken links. I applied minimal fix in [0e25472](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15694/commits/0e25472f033eb8eff30e2134241cefaaf5231725) (updated some links and removed if there are no relevant section anymore), but we should probably revise the "FastAPI People" page

---

Noticed that "Migrate from Pydantic v1 to Pydantic v2" page still states that FastAPI supports `pydantic.v1`, that is no longer true. Updated that page to clarify that it was only supported up to FastAPI 0.128.0 (commit [03b44b6](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15694/commits/03b44b64ce19fcd63035bd321ec4af5b3585b322))

---

Also fixed typos in agent skills (commit [0e25472](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/15694/commits/0e25472f033eb8eff30e2134241cefaaf5231725)) as it's also kind of documentation
author: YuriiMotov

review_comments.body:
Or
```suggestion
All this might seem contrived. And it might not yet be clear how it is useful.
```

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body: Claude said it should be `, as is the case with the user "entity" ...` (comma instead of period and added `is` verb), but it doesn't seem to read well to me and I asked to rephrase it to make it more readable

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
Or
```suggestion
There are many situations in which you need to notify a client that is using your API about an error.
```

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body: I understand the intention behind using "would have" and "had" here, but IMO it's a bit hard to read. I'd suggest simplifying it with the present tense.

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
Or

```suggestion
But `include_router` is still useful for, for example, adding options (like `dependencies`, `prefix`, and `tags`) when including a third party router, or a generic router that is shared between several projects.
```

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
Or
```suggestion
    * Up to now the application would run, and raise an exception only when receiving a request with form data, the new behavior, raising early, will prevent the developer from deploying applications with broken dependencies.
```

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
Laying is from **lay**, lying is from **lie**.
See https://share.google/aimode/j5ok5TVs4U7oAoSGo

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
**Temporal** means "related to concept of time".
**Temporary** - opposite of **permanent**.
See: https://share.google/aimode/p0RZVRHUxXv4Ier82

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
Or
```suggestion
It can be convenient to quickly access HTTP (and WebSocket) status codes in your app, using autocompletion for the name without having to remember the integer status codes by heart.
```

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
There are plenty of such mistakes.
See rule: https://share.google/aimode/OrqUJfjTvZbwqbpfz

review_comments.author: tiangolo
review_comments.body: Ah! I hadn't realized. But then in this case it's laying.

review_comments.author: YuriiMotov
review_comments.body:
I still think it's **lying**.

Link got stale so quickly.. Here is new link: https://share.google/aimode/UAHoIVGVzVZfEFHbb

> Use laying when putting or placing an object down. Use lying when referring to a person or thing resting horizontally, or when telling an untruth

**Laying** should always go with object (Laying something)

review_comments.author: tiangolo
review_comments.body: Sounds good!

review_comments.author: tiangolo
review_comments.body: Ah! You're right, I was thinking about only "telling a lie, something false", not about resting there. Nice, makes sense, thank you!

title: 🔨 Use `gpt-5.5` model in `translate.py`, specify `-chat` to avoid warnings
body:
The `-chat` suffix is needed to avoid the warning:
```
/home/yurii/code/1.FastAPI/fastapi/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pydantic_ai/agent/__init__.py:414: PydanticAIDeprecationWarning: In v2.0, 'openai:' will resolve to the OpenAI Responses API by default. Use 'openai-chat:' to keep current Chat Completions behavior, or 'openai-responses:' to opt in early.
```
author: YuriiMotov

title: ♻️ Refactor internals to preserve `APIRouter` and `APIRoute` instances
body:
## Pull Request

♻️ Refactor internals to preserve `APIRouter` and `APIRoute` instances

Supersedes https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/4794

Unblocks :sparkles: SO MANY THINGS :sparkles: 

Before this, `router.include_router(other_router)` would take each path operation from `other_router` and "clone" it, or recreate it from scratch.

This would mean that in the end there was only one top level router, part of the app.

The way it is structured here is that there are a few additional classes to handle intermediate metadata for router and route inclusion. That way the information of "router X includes Y and Y includes Z" is stored somewhere, without affecting (recreating / clonning) the final route.

### Non Objective

Dependencies for 404

Originally in the other PR I intended to support dependencies that would be executed even for 404, but that would conflict with the fact that a router could _not_ find a match, but the next router _did_ find a match. Executing dependencies in the router that did not find a match would not make sense, they could consume the request, body, etc.

This original idea was discarded.

### Breaking Change

Now `router.routes` is no longer a plain list of `APIRoute` objects, it can contain these intermediate objects that can contain additional routers, forming a tree.

Any logic that depended on iterating on the `router.routes` directly would be affected, that logic cannot expect to be able to extract data from a plain list of routes, as it's no longer a plain list but a tree.

Additionally, any logic that iterated on `router.routes` to modify them would now also see these new objects, and would not see all the routes in the app.

`router.routes` should be considered an internal implementation detail, only passed around to the FastAPI functions that need it.

### Features

* Adding routes after a router is included now works, they are reflected as they are not copied.
* Including `subrouter` in `mainrouter` can be done before adding routes (path operations) to `subrouter`, because now the the entire object is stored instead of copying the routes.
* As routes are not copied, in some cases that might save some memory.

### Alpha Features

This is not documented yet, so it's not officially supported yet and could change in the future.

But, as `APIRoute` and `APIRouter` instances are now preserved, they could be customized.

`APIRouter` has two new methods, `.matches()` and `.handle()`, counterpart to the existing ones in `APIRoute`. With this a router could customize how it matches and handles requests. For example, it could match only requests that include some specific header, for example for handling versions in headers.

Still, for now, consider this very experimental and potentially changing and breaking in the future.

### Future Features Enabled

* Custom `APIRoute` subclasses (as desccribed above)
* Custom `APIRouter` subclasses (as described above)
* Dependencies per router
* Exception handlers per router
* Middleware per router
* Other features planned

comments.body:
## 🤖 AI PR Review 摘要

**风险评分**: 56/100 (🟠 high) | **预计审查时间**: ~32 分钟

### 📝 变更摘要
此PR为FastAPI测试套件添加了路径参数验证的全面测试，覆盖了min_length、max_length、gt、lt、ge、le等约束条件，以及它们的组合（如lt-gt、le-ge）。同时为整数和浮点数类型分别添加了测试端点。变更涉及tests/test_path.py和tests/main.py两个文件，新增235行代码，旨在提升路径参数验证功能的测试覆盖率。

### ⚠️ 风险项 (17)

- 🟡 **tests/main.py/L61**: 在get_path_param_min_length函数中，Path参数使用了...（Ellipsis）作为默认值，但min_length=3。根据Pydantic v2，min_length约束在路径参数中可能不会按预期工作，因为路径参数默认是必需的，使用...可能冗余，但不会导致错误。然而，如果未来FastAPI或Pydantic版本改变行为，可能导致测试失败。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，直接使用Path(min_length=3)即可，因为路径参数默认是必需的。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L66**: 在get_path_param_max_length函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，与min_length类似，使用...是冗余的。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(max_length=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L71**: 在get_path_param_min_max_length函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，同样冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(max_length=3, min_length=2)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L76**: 在get_path_param_gt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L81**: 在get_path_param_gt0函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=0)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L86**: 在get_path_param_ge函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(ge=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L91**: 在get_path_param_lt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L96**: 在get_path_param_lt0函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=0)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L101**: 在get_path_param_le函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L106**: 在get_path_param_lt_gt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3, gt=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L111**: 在get_path_param_le_ge函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3, ge=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L116**: 在get_path_param_lt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L121**: 在get_path_param_gt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L126**: 在get_path_param_le_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L131**: 在get_path_param_ge_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(ge=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L136**: 在get_path_param_lt_gt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3, gt=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L141**: 在get_path_param_le_ge_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3, ge=1)。

### 💡 改进建议 (5)

- 🔧 **tests/test_path.py**: 测试数据中硬编码了错误响应字典，这些字典与Pydantic验证错误格式紧密耦合。如果Pydantic升级或更改错误消息格式，这些测试将需要大量更新。建议使用更灵活的方式验证错误响应，例如仅检查状态码和错误类型，而不是完整的错误消息。
  ```
  def test_path_param_minlength_foo():
    response = client.get("/path/param-minlength/foo")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == "foo"

def test_path_param_minlength_fo():
    response = client.get("/path/param-minlength/fo")
    assert response.status_code == 422
    # 仅验证错误类型和字段，不验证完整消息
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["type"] == "value_error.any_str.min_length"
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["loc"] == ["path", "item_id"]
  ```
- ✅ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试函数使用了参数化测试，但测试数据中混合了成功和失败用例。建议将成功和失败测试分开，以提高可读性和可维护性。
  ```
  # 成功用例
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_response",
    [
        ("/path/param-minlength/foo", "foo"),
        ("/path/param-maxlength/foo", "foo"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path_success(path, expected_response):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == expected_response

# 失败用例
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_error_type",
    [
        ("/path/param-minlength/fo", "value_error.any_str.min_length"),
        ("/path/param-maxlength/foobar", "value_error.any_str.max_length"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path_failure(path, expected_error_type):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == 422
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["type"] == expected_error_type
  ```
- 🔧 **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中缺少对边界值的测试，例如对于gt=3，测试了2（失败）和42（成功），但未测试3本身（边界值）。建议添加边界值测试以确保验证逻辑正确。
  ```
  ("/path/param-gt/3", 422, response_greater_than_3),  # 边界值，应失败
("/path/param-ge/3", 200, 3),  # 边界值，应成功
("/path/param-lt/3", 422, response_less_than_3),  # 边界值，应失败
("/path/param-le/3", 200, 3),  # 边界值，应成功
  ```
- ✅ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中使用了response_not_valid_int变量，但在提供的代码片段中未定义该变量。请确保在测试文件中定义了该变量，否则测试将失败。
  ```
  response_not_valid_int = {
    "detail": [
        {
            "type": "int_parsing",
            "loc": ["path", "item_id"],
            "msg": "Input should be a valid integer, unable to parse string as an integer",
            "input": "2.7",
        }
    ]
}
  ```
- ⚡ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中使用了多个独立的测试函数（如test_text_get、test_nonexistent等），这些函数可以合并到参数化测试中，以减少代码重复并提高执行效率。
  ```
  @pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_status,expected_response",
    [
        ("/text", 200, "Hello World"),
        ("/nonexistent", 404, {"detail": "Not Found"}),
        ("/path/foobar", 200, "foobar"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path(path, expected_status, expected_response):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == expected_status
    assert response.json() == expected_response
  ```

title: ♻️ Validate Server Sent Event fields to avoid applications from sending broken data
body:
## Pull Request

<!--
Please start with a GitHub Discussion.

Once a team member asks you to open a PR, create it and link the discussion here.

Obvious typo fixes can be made in a PR without starting a discussion.
-->

Discussion: <!-- Link to the GitHub Discussion -->

## Description

♻️ Validate Server Sent Event fields to avoid applications from sending broken data

This was reported multiple times as a "vulnerability" by users probably using automated AI tools, with the argument that if input was taken from the request and passed directly to an event unfiltered, it could break the client.

If an app sets an `id` or `event` with multi-line content, I would consider that app broken, and probably the app itself vulnerable to something, not FastAPI. With this change, if any app sets invalid multi-line values (sourced from unfiltered input or in any other way), FastAPI will make it a server error (which it is, as the app is sending invalid data).

I don't think the code before this PR has a bug, less a vulnerability. This change is mainly a quality of life improvement to prevent badly written apps from shooting themselves in the foot by passing unsanitized data directly to a SSE.

title: ⚡️ Speed up test suite via caching and fixture scopes to make it ~24% faster
body:
While running the tests, I noticed a couple of spots where we could speed things up a bit based on profiling.

1.  **Caching `verify_password`:**
    *   The `verify_password` helper (using bcrypt) can be a bit slow, and I saw it was getting called with the same inputs multiple times during the test run.
    *   I added `@lru_cache()` to it. Since the results are always the same for the same inputs, this is safe.
    *   In my run, this cut its calls from ~70 down to ~40, and its share of the total test time dropped from **~26.9%** to **~16.2%**.

2.  **Optimizing Fixture Scopes:**
    *   I also saw two fixtures that were being set up and torn down *a lot* (like 166 and 86 times!), taking up around **8.7%** and **4.9%** of the total test time respectively.
    *   Since they didn't seem to need a completely fresh state for *every single test function*, I changed their scope to `scope="module"`.
    *   Now they run way less often, and their time usage basically disappeared from the profile.

**Overall Speedup:**

The profiling breakdown suggested these specific parts contributed to a relative reduction of ~24.3% of the original time.

**Actual measured impact:** Running the full test suite (2334 passed, 130 skipped) on my machine took **24.37s** before these changes and now takes **16.85s**.

That's a time saving of 7.52s, resulting in an overall test suite speedup of **~30.9%** (`(24.37 - 16.85) / 24.37 * 100%`).

Hopefully, this makes the test suite noticeably faster for everyone! Let me know what you think. 🙂

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3bae3789-debd-4366-ac1c-9e7263d09653)

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/53639ddf-c5a3-40df-a448-d0f32df8bbe5)

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/40eb338d-2b4c-4aa1-a43d-a23ff1df2136)

comments.author: nidhishgajjar
comments.body:
**Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM 5.1 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Review of PR #13583: Speed up test suite via caching and fixture scopes

Thanks for this well-motivated optimization! The profiling data and measured speedup (~30%) make a strong case. Here are my observations:

### What looks good ✅

- **Proper cleanup**: The `cache_verify_password` fixture correctly restores the original function after yielding, preventing test pollution.
- **Engine disposal**: Adding `mod.engine.dispose()` in the SQL tests is good practice for releasing database connections.
- **Well-scoped changes**: All changes are confined to test code, so no production risk.
- **Clear profiling data**: The before/after screenshots and measured times (24.37s → 16.85s) are compelling.

### Minor observations 💡

1. **Monkey-patching `verify_password`**: The approach of replacing `mod.verify_password` with a cached version works because `lru_cache` keys on input arguments, so it remains correct even if future tests add new user credentials. This is safe.

2. **`scope="module"` and shared database state**: With `get_client` now module-scoped, `clear_sqlmodel()` runs once per module instead of per test. This is fine as long as the test functions within each module don't require independent clean database states. Looking at `test_crud_app`, it tests a full CRUD sequence in a single function, so there shouldn't be inter-test interference.

3. **`cache_verify_password` assert**: The `assert hasattr(mod, 'verify_password')` is a nice defensive check. Consider using `pytest.skip()` instead of a bare `assert` — a hard failure might be confusing if this fixture is accidentally included in a module that doesn't have `verify_password`. That said, since it's in a specific test file, this is fine as-is.

### Summary

A solid, well-measured optimization to the test suite with proper cleanup and no production impact. The ~30% speedup is a nice win for developer experience.

**Assessment: approve**

### 📝 变更摘要
此PR为FastAPI测试套件添加了路径参数验证的全面测试，覆盖了min_length、max_length、gt、lt、ge、le等约束条件，以及它们的组合（如lt-gt、le-ge）。同时为整数和浮点数类型分别添加了测试端点。变更涉及tests/test_path.py和tests/main.py两个文件，新增235行代码，旨在提升路径参数验证功能的测试覆盖率。

### ⚠️ 风险项 (17)

- 🟡 **tests/main.py/L61**: 在get_path_param_min_length函数中，Path参数使用了...（Ellipsis）作为默认值，但min_length=3。根据Pydantic v2，min_length约束在路径参数中可能不会按预期工作，因为路径参数默认是必需的，使用...可能冗余，但不会导致错误。然而，如果未来FastAPI或Pydantic版本改变行为，可能导致测试失败。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，直接使用Path(min_length=3)即可，因为路径参数默认是必需的。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L66**: 在get_path_param_max_length函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，与min_length类似，使用...是冗余的。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(max_length=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L71**: 在get_path_param_min_max_length函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，同样冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(max_length=3, min_length=2)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L76**: 在get_path_param_gt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L81**: 在get_path_param_gt0函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=0)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L86**: 在get_path_param_ge函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(ge=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L91**: 在get_path_param_lt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L96**: 在get_path_param_lt0函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=0)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L101**: 在get_path_param_le函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L106**: 在get_path_param_lt_gt函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3, gt=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L111**: 在get_path_param_le_ge函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3, ge=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L116**: 在get_path_param_lt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L121**: 在get_path_param_gt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(gt=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L126**: 在get_path_param_le_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L131**: 在get_path_param_ge_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(ge=3)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L136**: 在get_path_param_lt_gt_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(lt=3, gt=1)。
- 🟢 **tests/main.py/L141**: 在get_path_param_le_ge_int函数中，Path参数使用了...作为默认值，冗余。
  > 💡 修复建议: 移除...默认值，使用Path(le=3, ge=1)。

### 💡 改进建议 (5)

- 🔧 **tests/test_path.py**: 测试数据中硬编码了错误响应字典，这些字典与Pydantic验证错误格式紧密耦合。如果Pydantic升级或更改错误消息格式，这些测试将需要大量更新。建议使用更灵活的方式验证错误响应，例如仅检查状态码和错误类型，而不是完整的错误消息。
  ```
  def test_path_param_minlength_foo():
    response = client.get("/path/param-minlength/foo")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == "foo"

def test_path_param_minlength_fo():
    response = client.get("/path/param-minlength/fo")
    assert response.status_code == 422
    # 仅验证错误类型和字段，不验证完整消息
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["type"] == "value_error.any_str.min_length"
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["loc"] == ["path", "item_id"]
  ```
- ✅ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试函数使用了参数化测试，但测试数据中混合了成功和失败用例。建议将成功和失败测试分开，以提高可读性和可维护性。
  ```
  # 成功用例
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_response",
    [
        ("/path/param-minlength/foo", "foo"),
        ("/path/param-maxlength/foo", "foo"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path_success(path, expected_response):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == expected_response

# 失败用例
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_error_type",
    [
        ("/path/param-minlength/fo", "value_error.any_str.min_length"),
        ("/path/param-maxlength/foobar", "value_error.any_str.max_length"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path_failure(path, expected_error_type):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == 422
    assert response.json()["detail"][0]["type"] == expected_error_type
  ```
- 🔧 **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中缺少对边界值的测试，例如对于gt=3，测试了2（失败）和42（成功），但未测试3本身（边界值）。建议添加边界值测试以确保验证逻辑正确。
  ```
  ("/path/param-gt/3", 422, response_greater_than_3),  # 边界值，应失败
("/path/param-ge/3", 200, 3),  # 边界值，应成功
("/path/param-lt/3", 422, response_less_than_3),  # 边界值，应失败
("/path/param-le/3", 200, 3),  # 边界值，应成功
  ```
- ✅ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中使用了response_not_valid_int变量，但在提供的代码片段中未定义该变量。请确保在测试文件中定义了该变量，否则测试将失败。
  ```
  response_not_valid_int = {
    "detail": [
        {
            "type": "int_parsing",
            "loc": ["path", "item_id"],
            "msg": "Input should be a valid integer, unable to parse string as an integer",
            "input": "2.7",
        }
    ]
}
  ```
- ⚡ **tests/test_path.py**: 测试中使用了多个独立的测试函数（如test_text_get、test_nonexistent等），这些函数可以合并到参数化测试中，以减少代码重复并提高执行效率。
  ```
  @pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "path,expected_status,expected_response",
    [
        ("/text", 200, "Hello World"),
        ("/nonexistent", 404, {"detail": "Not Found"}),
        ("/path/foobar", 200, "foobar"),
        # ...
    ],
)
def test_get_path(path, expected_status, expected_response):
    response = client.get(path)
    assert response.status_code == expected_status
    assert response.json() == expected_response
  ```

title: Add Open API prefix route - correct docs behind reverse proxy
body:
I run a lot of services in Kubernetes using [Ambassador](https://www.getambassador.io/) as my API Gateway. Ambassador allows you to configure a route prefix for each one of your services and then it handles ingress and routing to the initial service. This PR enables me to configure the same prefix I use with Ambassador in the Swagger UI API docs.

Example:
```python
prefix='/service1'

app = FastAPI(
    openapi_prefix=prefix
)
@app.get('/something') => SwaggerRoute (GET - /service1/something)
```

NOTE: This is not the same as using the include_router method and adding a prefix.
This is meant only to rewrite the route paths in the Swagger UI.

BTW: Awesome Project! Thanks so much for your work on this, I'm using fastapi a ton these days.

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body: I know this is an old issue/PR, but shouldn't `root_path` be used instead of adding a custom parameter to the application? `root_path` is already the intended mechanism for routing under a mounted subapplication and what Uvicorn and the ASGI spec in general uses to tell the root application what its URL prefix is (it is ignored when routing, but must be taken into account when reconstructing URLs). FastAPI [currently does not acknowledge `root_path` in its API documentation](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/90236c813550030040474a7714428cf1fcc4f582/fastapi/openapi/utils.py#L283-L291), only  `openapi_prefix`, which means the routes specified by the API doc and the ones that are actually understood by the application will not match unless the value of `root_path` is also passed as `openapi_prefix` when creating the FastAPI application.

comments.author: luckydonald
comments.body: Huh, sounds about right. @sm-Fifteen you may open a new issue for that.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Yeap @sm-Fifteen, I just merged https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1199 addressing `root_path` :rocket:

title: Fix SQLAlchemy support with ORM
body:
Fix SQLAlchemy support, including ORM. And update docs accordingly.

* Update `jsonable_encoder` to support SQLAlchemy models by default, with a new `sqlalchemy_safe` parameter that checks for keys specific to SQLAlchemy.
* Update tutorial for SQLAlchemy, update code and documentation.
* Use a simple SQLite database for the tutorial, to make it very fast to replicate, while allowing to change a URL to switch to PostgreSQL (or others).
* Add tests for SQLAlchemy tutorial code.
* The new code example can be run as is (copy-paste-run), with a local SQLite database.

This is related to #4 (and it should fix it), might be related to #27 (as the current tutorial is broken). This new tutorial, fix and code should simplify starting with SQLAlchemy.

At some point, it could make sense to add (or replace) instructions for Starlette's integrated database utils, or the new https://github.com/encode/databases and PR #20.

Let's see how it all evolves. For now, this should work in a simple way, supporting SQLAlchemy ORM (that is not possible otherwise). This should also make it easier for people coming from mainstream frameworks like Flask and Django.

title: APIRouter tags
body: Humble attempt at solving #54
author: euri10

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Awesome! That's a lot of work you put in there! :clap: :taco: 

---

I was thinking that we could put this logic in the `.include_router()` method.

We could change this line: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L240

And add a parameter `tags: List[str] = None`.

And then change this line: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L253

To something like:

`tags=(route.tags or []) + (tags or []),`

---

That way we don't have to add extra code to the path operation decorators and especially, we don't have to override the `APIRouter.__init__()` method. By not overriding it, it's a lot easier to support new versions of Starlette that can change it.

title: Additional Responses
body:
#### Additional Responses Support

**Summary**

allows additional responses to be defined beside the default one

**Example**

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import uuid, datetime as dt

import fastapi
from starlette import responses
import pydantic as dantic

from fastapi.openapi import models, constants

app = fastapi.FastAPI(debug=True)


class Response400(dantic.BaseModel):
    '''HTTP 4xx responses schema'''
    summary: str
    description: str
    ref_code: str # functional error ref


class Point(dantic.BaseModel):
    id: dantic.UUID1
    latitude: float
    longitude: float
    pinned: bool
    pinned_at: dt.datetime


response_403 = models.AdditionalResponse(
    status_code=403,
    description='Forbidden',
    models=[
        Response400,
    ],
)

response_500 = models.AdditionalResponse(
    status_code=500,
    description='Server Error',
    content_type='text/plain',
)


@app.post(
    '/pin-my-location',
    summary='Pin My Location',
    description='Pin my location ...',
    response_model=Point,
    additional_responses=[
        response_403,
        response_500,
    ])
async def pin_my_location():
    if dt.datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y-%m') != '2019-03':
        return responses.UJSONResponse({
            'summary': 'Access Denied!',
            'detail': 'No Access allowed after Mar 2019.',
            'ref_code': 'TNQ00723',
        },
        status_code=403,)
    return Point(
        id=uuid.uuid1(7, 3),
        latitude=0.0,
        longitude=0.0,
        pinned=True,
        pinned_at=dt.datetime.utcnow(),
    )
```
**Result**

![Screen Shot 2019-03-22 at 10 44 42 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2647889/54849722-2355c480-4cf6-11e9-8085-e8b98dd34b6a.png)

you can also define additional responses on `app.include_router` so it can save you from repeating the definition of common response types (e.g. `HTTP 500`)

**Limitations**

This pull request only implements the injection of
additional responses definitions into OpenAPI schema
to be appeared in swagger or other OpenAPI 3 compatible
clients (e.g. postman), here are some of the limitations:

- No validation on additional responses if they were returned.
- No support for multiple media types in response content definition.
- Extra fields on response not implemented yet (e.g. `headers` & `examples`).
author: barsi

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Thanks! I'll review it soon.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @barsi, I see you put quite some effort into this, thanks for that! :clap: :cake: 

---

I took it further and updated/refactored it all to make it simpler for developers to use while extending the capabilities.

For example, it's possible to add more info to an existing `response_model`, like `example`. And they get the data from the additional response and the model combined.

I also included docs, tests for the code in the docs, etc.

With this you can now add a `responses` parameter with a `dict`, e.g.:

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse


class Item(BaseModel):
    id: str
    value: str


class Message(BaseModel):
    message: str


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/{item_id}", response_model=Item, responses={404: {"model": Message}})
async def read_item(item_id: str):
    if item_id == "foo":
        return {"id": "foo", "value": "there goes my hero"}
    else:
        return JSONResponse(status_code=404, content={"message": "Item not found"})
```

The new docs are here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/additional-responses/

title: Static swagger
body:
About #110 

it adds an optional `static_directory` to FastAPI, if it's None (which it is by default) the generated html uses the cdn existing at present and the icon also.
If it's set to a path, path is added as a starlette StaticFile, and in that case, FastAPI needs an extra dictionary with js, css and favicon

I'd rather discuss if you're ok with that implementation before doing /redoc :see_no_evil: , some stuff might be moved in `setup()`

~and I'm struggling with those mypy errors, would love some help !~
author: euri10

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
First, thanks for your work @euri10 !

I have a couple of concerns:

The compiled JS code is almost 1 MB, and at each new Swagger UI version, we would need to update it. And the changes would be huge, as it is not the source code but the "compiled" (transpiled/minified) version.

I think one way to solve it could be to add a script for building the package, that pulls the lastest JS version from NPM (or any CDN) and adds it to the dist files. But I'm still not completely sure how to make Flit "bundle" it, and then how to add it/serve it in FastAPI, being a file included as part of the `fastapi` package itself.

Another option would be to create an additional optional package, that includes those static JS files, and can be installed and used with FastAPI.

Let's keep this on hold, I'll investigate what options do we have, and what's the best/most clean way to solve this use case.

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
I'm not sure I understand your concern, I surely miss something :)

It's just an option users will have:

- either passing nothing aka `app=FastAPI()` which defaults to the cdn version of the files.
- either pass a `static_directory` kwarg and a dict with the 3 relevant files aka `app = FastAPI(static_directory=static_directory, swagger_static={ "js": swagger_static_js, "css": swagger_static_css, "favicon": swagger_static_icon,},)`

If the css / js changes today, you'll update the 2 links in the docs.py file:

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/08d849d5c588f9cf14a38ede9e9d4d0b29a6d9c2/fastapi/openapi/docs.py#L10
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/08d849d5c588f9cf14a38ede9e9d4d0b29a6d9c2/fastapi/openapi/docs.py#L21

Should that option be implemented you'll change them in the spot they're defined by default, I don't see why you'd need to ship them with the library

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Ah! Sorry, I was misunderstanding something.

There's an actual copy of the Swagger UI JS bundle in the tests. I was thinking that it was meant to be included as the default static version.

---

As this approach would still require users (developers) to download and configure the static files by hand and then pass additional parameters to the app object, it would be quite some effort to set everything up. So, most of the effort required in the original issue #110 would still be needed.

I think I would prefer to:

* Document how to serve static.
* Document how to send HTML responses.
* Document how to combine the two to override the docs.

---

Then, in the case of #110, to reduce as much as possible the steps to get static local docs, I think it could be done with an external installable Python package that includes those static files and can be connected somehow to the app. But I'm not sure yet about all the details.

It seems to be very close to what I think we could use, the drawback I see is that it's several versions behind. But still, it seems promising.

title: Add auto_error to security utils
body:
:sparkles: Add `auto_error` to security utils to allow them to be optional, also allowing the declaration of multiple security schemes.

---

When setting `auto_error=False`, instead of raising/returning an HTTP 403 "Not authenticated" error to the client, it returns `None` to the parameter in a *path operation function* or dependency.

This makes it possible to have optional authentication.

It also allows to declare multiple optional security schemes in a dependency. And then, inside of the dependency, enforce that at least one is provided/validated. This can be used when allowing authentication with username/password using `OAuth2PasswordBearer` but also with other OAuth2 providers (Google, Facebook, etc).

To use it, in the security utils, instead of writing:

```Python
oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/token")
```

write:

```Python
oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="/token", auto_error=False)
```

It can also be used to customize the error returned, overwriting the default "Not authenticated" with something different, by taking the result of the dependency and if it's `None`, raising an `HTTPException` with a custom `detail` message.

So, guys, actually `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock` are irrelevant for the built package. They are there only for local development of FastAPI itself.

The only authoritative place that says what are the dependencies is the `pyproject.toml`.

Updating it in the `Pipfile` is helpful so that the local development environment reflects what is being defined in the official dependencies. But otherwise, it doesn't affect the final package.

It's mounted to the app under `/{docs_url}/oauth2-redirect`

This is needed for implicit grant Oauth2 flow in swagger ui, so it can grab the token on redirect, e.g. this kind of security scheme:

```python
from fastapi.security import OAuth2
from fastapi.openapi.models import OAuthFlows, OAuthFlowImplicit

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2(
    flows=OAuthFlows(
        implicit=OAuthFlowImplicit(
            authorizationUrl="https://some/oauth2/provider/login/url"
        )
    )
)
```

title: Add param functions, to override types, to make mypy happy
body:
:sparkles: Add param functions, to override types, to make `mypy` happy.

This would solve https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/219 and other cases of `mypy` complaining about the default types.

## Simple solution

The simplest approach would be to make `mypy` ignore the types in those parameters, using comments with `# type: ignore` like in:

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Path, Query

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
async def read_items(  # type: ignore
    item_id: int = Path(..., title="The ID of the item to get"),
    q: str = Query(None, alias="item-query"),  
):
    results = {"item_id": item_id}
    if q:
        results.update({"q": q})
    return results
```

### Drawback

But asking all the users to add comments to ignore type checks each time they use these classes would be difficult and cumbersome (for end users/developers).

## Reason

The issue is that `mypy` is right, from the typing point of view. Putting an instance of `Query` as the "default value" of a parameter with type `str` is not really correct (from the typing point of view).

But here in FastAPI we are "hacking" the typing system, and extending its use to the extreme (which has proven to work really well for many users/use-cases). The *path operation function* is only called by FastAPI (not by the user or any other code), and FastAPI makes sure to pass the correct type, those classes are only for meta-information, used by FastAPI.

## Alternatives

I tried overriding the type declaration of the parameter classes, but there's no way to achieve that.

Then I tried adding a typing stub (`parameters.pyi`), but although it removed `mypy` errors, there where other errors in places used by FastAPI internally.

And then, by relaxing the type declarations to avoid the internal errors, the type hints of those parameters were also lost for final users.

There's a lot of work (and code) there just to have completion for all those parameters in classes... One of the main objectives is for FastAPI to be as comfortable for final developers as possible, even if that is at the expense of the developers of the library itself (me and the community that submits PRs :grin: ).

## Final solution

Here's the solution. We can't override the type declaration of a class (the class itself).

But we can override the return type declaration of a function.

So, I converted all these parameter classes exposed by FastAPI (`Depends`, `Query`, `File`, etc) to functions. But then, as the function is actually called when the code is run and FastAPI needs to inspect it afterwards when the app is running, each of these functions returns the original class instances.

So, the default values of the parameters are actually those classes, and can be checked by the rest of the FastAPI code (dependencies, etc).

title: upgrade pydantic to 0.28
body:
This fixes #307 and #308 
Looks like the change to pydantic is required due to their changes to support circular JSON Schema references 
https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/commit/d73aa1bded92efbab48832c9589391b9c1b4d17c#diff-7d11a8c77c4167b2958bcdb27b18cf2a

There is a decent chance I may be missing something, but simply passing an empty set for known_models passes all tests. 


A better solution might be for pydantic to change the signature of `get_flat_models_from_fields` to 
```python
def get_flat_models_from_field(field: Field, known_models: Set[Type['BaseModel']] = None) -> Set[Type['BaseModel']]:
    known_models = known_models or set()
    flat_models: Set[Type['BaseModel']] = set()
    # Handle dataclass-based models
    field_type = field.type_
    if lenient_issubclass(getattr(field_type, '__pydantic_model__', None), pydantic.BaseModel):
        field_type = field_type.__pydantic_model__  # type: ignore
    if field.sub_fields:
        flat_models |= get_flat_models_from_fields(field.sub_fields, known_models=known_models)
    elif lenient_issubclass(field_type, pydantic.BaseModel) and field_type not in known_models:
        flat_models |= get_flat_models_from_model(field_type, known_models=known_models)
    return flat_models
```

title: Remove Pipfile.lock from the repository
body:
Remove `Pipfile.lock` from the repository.

Being a library, it should work independently of locking of the dependency tree.

The `Pipfile` (and `Pipfile.lock`) is only used locally for development of FastAPI itself, it doesn't affect final users (that is controlled with `pyproject.toml`).

The `Pipfile.lock` adds unnecessary noise to PRs that update/upgrade development packages, and the locking is not the same in all environments (e.g. Linux, Mac, and Windows).

Each FastAPI contributor (developing FastAPI itself) can have his/her own `Pipfile.lock`, but it doesn't have to be in the git repo.

title: Fix same function names in different modules with composite bodies
body:
Fix same function names in different modules with composite bodies.

Currently, if two *path operation functions* are named the same, in two separate modules, and receive several body parameters, they are converted into a composite body JSON Schema for OpenAPI.

But the names are generated based on the *path operation* name, and if the name is not assigned but autogenerated (based on the function name), two JSON Schemas could have the same name, breaking the OpenAPI generation.

This PR re-implements the name generation for composite bodies and response JSON Schemas in OpenAPI.

An explicit example can be found in the added tests.

This also means that it's now possible to use `.include_router` multiple times with the *same* router, under different `prefix`es, and it will work. For example, it can be used to expose the same router under different API prefixes (e.g. `/api/v2` and `/api/latest`).

This fixes #284


comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @steinitzu I'm assuming it's just a way to ensure you don't accidentally end up with paths that aren't separated by slashes. Either way, to @euri10's point, I think that the right place to ask this question would probably be on `starlette` because of this: https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/routing.py#L147

comments.author: vitalik
dmontagu
comments.body:
@vitalik 
> well starlette routing have nothing to do with fast-API app.get/post/patch

I think that's a little extreme; clearly they are related -- `APIRoute` is a subclass of `starlette.routing.Route` after all.

That said, it is a valid point that `include_router` doesn't exist in starlette, so this concern wouldn't apply. Given `APIRoute` is typically added to an app via `include_router` I'm convinced this is a reasonable change. I personally would be fine with merging it.

Either way, it would certainly be less controversial if there was a way to implement this that didn't remove validation that would be typically be performed in the superclass.

comments.author: vitalik
comments.body:
I'm sorry - maybe I am extreme , sorry about that - but this patch is only removing one line with assert inside fastapi code (have nothing to do with starlette) that's it (plus extra check that both include an route are not empty)

if you can give me any other option how to make my code more modular/isolated withot this assert - I would be happy to use it

comments.author: vitalik
comments.body:
Hi @tiangolo 
Can you share your concerns or plans about this PR ?

Thank you

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thank you @vitalik ! Merged! :tada: :rocket: 

---

I think I can agree with you all :smile: 

I agree I don't want to diverge from Starlette, but it's true that the router functionality and the "path operations" are already handled differently.

I think for a new developer to find a path operation with an empty route in an existing codebase could be confusing, and that in some cases it could be more "intuitive" to have paths that are a prefix of other paths always have a trailing slash.

But that's a bit subjective, depending on the point of view, in fact, GitHub itself has by default the contrary behavior, the one that would be supported by this feature (it's https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi , not https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/).

I also see that this is a feature that seems to help several use cases (at least 3, just from this thread), and I think it might help especially with some migrations, to keep paths consistent with previous implementations. Also, the flexibility it adds doesn't break current behavior, so I think it's fine.

Thanks for your contribution @vitalik ! And thanks for the interest and discussion here everyone!

---

Now, although I ran the tests locally first (and they passed), as Travis seems to have not triggered, I see now there's a mypy error after merging :disappointed:. So I'll fix it before a release.

title: Use caching logic to determine openapi spec
body:
Dependencies that resolve to the same cache key are not re-used while building the openapi schema.

Addresses #416
author: dmontagu

comments.author: dmontagu
henriklindgren
comments.body: @dmontagu have you considered if this affects https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/header-params/#duplicate-headers?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@henriklindgren That's a good question, but I think this is still safe -- it only modifies things when calling the *exact same* dependency *function*. If you were to list a header argument multiple times in *different* dependencies, it would still get added multiple times.

If I understand correctly, any schema that would be changed by this PR is probably invalid right now (since it would be repeating arguments with the same names).

comments.author: henriklindgren
comments.body:
Ah true, read through it all now, agreed. The way I noticed the bug was from generating a client which broke from it, I would be surprised if people would use it this way on purpose so it should be safe.

>  if you were to have the same callable as a dependency in multiple places with different security schemes, you'd get a repeat of the dependency's arguments in the schema for each different security scheme

Let's see if I've understood this part correctly. It seems safe to ignore this case because it would be really awkward if multiple security schemes in the same context based key values on the order they arrived, dependent on other schemes. It honestly feels safer to know that you would get the single argument and be sure of it. Otherwise you'd use argument lists as mentioned (such as the duplicate header list), which could be used as a workaround if you really want this behaviour.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Great! Thanks @dmontagu for the thorough investigation and explanation, here and in the issue. :cake: :rocket:

---

I think we can have this merged as is right now.

Combining the scopes of the same security schemes could make sense, but that would mean "in OpenAPI terms" that *all* the scopes are required. And the intention could be to have *any* of them required. That's actually not enforced by FastAPI but by the implementation itself. If the user/developer leaves the `auto_error=True`, then *all* the security schemes would be required. But if he has `auto_error=False` and then some additional logic to decide if the operation is allowed, then it could be a different case.

This is a pretty advanced use case of OpenAPI, having multiple security schemes combined that some can be required or not.

I think we can leave it as-is (with this PR merged) for now and see if there's more logic required for that for some use case later.

title: Fix unskipped defaults
author: dmontagu

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Wow, you had to go quite deep into the rabbit hole to find that (`field.validate` and `BaseModel.validate` are quite deep inside the callback flow in Pydantic). Good job! Thanks! :clap: :rocket:

I updated the test to check for defaults in sub-models. And updated the implementation to use `value = response.dict(skip_defaults=skip_defaults)` to take into account those sub-models.

---

And yeah, it's unfortunate to have to do a second parse of the models and have to "clone" models. 

That came after adding Pydantic ORM mode, as now the data is serialized by Pydantic directly (including DB models). And a sub-model including something sensitive (e.g. a `hashed_password`) still passes validation as-is for the parent model. So, a model `class UserWithPassword(BaseUser)` passes validation for a model `class BaseUser(BaseModel)`, including the extra sensitive data.

To avoid that security issue, I had to "clone" the models declared in `response_model`, to make sure they are a "different" class, and sub-classes don't pass directly but have to be parsed and validated.

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body:
```python
        if isinstance(response, BaseModel):
            value = response.dict(skip_defaults=skip_defaults)
```
might also work, but I'm not sure if that would play differently with include/exclude/`jsonable_encoder`. And given `skip_defaults` is probably usually false, I think the version above will be more performant in most cases.

If merged, this issue fixes #428

Awesome, thanks @divums ! Good job! :rocket: :cake: 

I updated it a bit, to use uppercase instead of lowercase, to conform with the spec: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#patterned-fields-1

And I augmented the tests to include some more of these cases.

And thanks a lot @dmontagu for the code review and help here! :clap: :muscle: :cake:

title: Add support for strings and __future__ annotations
body: This pull request adds support for forward references, string annotations, and `from __future__ import annotations` in decorated endpoint signatures.
author: dmontagu

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Nice! Good job!

> This PR would introduce a small breaking change [...]

No problem with that. We bump the version to release it.

> Before this is ready to merge [...]

Perfect. Let me know when you can check those things.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@tiangolo Thanks for the feedback!

I actually discussed this with Ivan Levkivskyi on the python/typing gitter, and he suggested I make a bpo post (which I did here: https://bugs.python.org/issue37923). So far it hasn't gotten much attention 😕 but I figured I'd at least ask the question.

Having dug a little more into the various related PEPs and python stdlib source, I feel pretty good about the approach in this PR, so I'm inclined to run with it, and just add more edge-case handling and tests for them. Hopefully in the future there will be first class support for this built directly into the `inspect` or `typing` packages, and we can remove any custom/redundant logic.

I'll try to get this into a mergeable state this weekend.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @tiangolo I reworked the implementation, and now it should be 100% backward compatible (no changes to the annotations in the case of optionals, etc.), and only change any of the behavior when string-valued annotations are present. (And the only behavior change will be that it works instead of raising errors.)

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@tiangolo

* I made the requested changes.
* I also updated the `# type: ignore` comments to:
    1. be compatible with the recent mypy 0.730 release (which is automatically used in the build)
    2. remove unused ignores (detected by calling `mypy fastapi --disallow-untyped-defs --warn-unused-ignore`)
* Do you think the docs should be updated anywhere? It seems like release notes is probably enough

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body: I commented out a variety of edge-case checks here to ensure code coverage would pass, but before merging, these checks should be uncommented with appropriate tests added.

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body:
This file looked like a good example of complex dependency usage, and allowed me to get 100% coverage for tests.

@tiangolo How would you propose we handle testing this functionality?
* Should I copy this file (or another) and just add the `from __future__ import annotations` import?
* Should I create an entirely new file with a more comprehensive set of string-annotation tests?
    * In this case, is there a good place for me to look for examples?

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body:
I originally wanted to use `typing.get_type_hints` directly, but ultimately this implementation seemed cleaner and follows `typing.get_type_hints` where it matters (namely, using the same logic to determine the local and global namespaces, and essentially using the same logic to evaluate string references by converting them to forward references (and evaluating those forward references).

It also uses `pydantic.utils` to handle differences between the `typing` api for python 3.6 and 3.7 (for `ForwardRef` and `evaluate_forwardref` anyway), and to deal with the fact that the relevant objects are technically private in `typing`. Since `pydantic` has made them public, it seems reasonable to let `pydantic` worry about ensuring the public API is stable. Moreover, this ensures compatibility with the way pydantic handles forward references anyway.

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body: It might be better to make this change in another (possibly new) file; @tiangolo if you give me some direction I can implement the tests however you'd prefer.

title: Only use default parameter values for path parameters (#450)
body:
This change allows endpoint functions to be defined with parameters with defaults, but for those defaults to be ignored if the function parameter subsequently appears as a path parameter. The default value is only operative if the parameter is a query parameter.

This change includes a new set of tests for this functionality.

title: make it possible to change the default reponse_class
body:
now you can do app = FastAPI(default_response_class=UJSONResponse)

and all routes will use UJSONResponse by default.

You can still override response_class in routes as before.

the default is still kept as JSONResponse, just specified in much fewer places (two)
author: toppk

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
A use case for this change that I find especially compelling:

It would make it substantially easier to make use of a custom response that gets serialized (in middleware) as a function of request headers. (E.g., based on a request header, serialize to json, msgpack, protobuf, etc.). Currently that would require you to change the response_class *everywhere*; this seems like a pretty broadly-applicable use case since it is still useful even if you want to use json most of the time.

(See https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/521)

title: Ensure `skip_defaults` doesn't cause extra fields to be serialized
body:
Currently, if `skip_defaults` is true, the secure cloned field is not used when serializing the response. This can lead to extra information leaking out if the `response_model` differs in type from the returned model.

This pull request fixes this bug, and updates the relevant unit test to check for it.

(The bug was introduced in #422 -- sorry about that!)

-----

I believe this pull request also improves performance in the case where `skip_defaults` is `True`, as the response will now only be validated once in the `serialize_response` call, instead of twice.

title: Preserve route_class when calling include_router
body:
Ensures that the `route_class` of the subrouter is preserved when including a subrouter in a parent router.

------

Right now, when including a router with a custom `route_class`, the `route_class` is not automatically preserved. This essentially prevents the use of custom `route_class` without special tricks. (For example, see #521, where overriding the `route_class` is used to enable msgpack parsing.)

This PR changes the behavior of `include_router` so that the route class is preserved during inclusion for any routes that are subclasses of `APIRoute`.

-----

Closes #539
author: dmontagu

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @tiangolo I just re-ran the checks for this PR. Note #568 may serve as an alternative, but would require a little more effort. I'm not sure which way (if either) you'd be inclined to go.

Thanks @dmontagu ! Great! :rocket: :tada: 

I just added a couple of tests to make sure we avoid possible future regressions with this. :white_check_mark:

comments.body: Thanks, sorry the PR wasn't more complete!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
No worries at all, you're doing an awesome job helping with FastAPI here. With code and also helping others in issues and in Gitter.

You rock! :guitar: :rocket: :tada: :bowing_man:

comments.author: ifigueroap
comments.body:
Hi, I'm wondering on whether it is possible to externally override the `route_class` of an included router. A use case would be to add a logging implementation (via subclassing of `APIRoute`) to existing routers. This is currently possible only if the source is available, and only by doing it before adding the routes, but it appears not to be possible for external modules. The `route_class_override` parameter is also only available at the actual call of `add_api_route`.

The crux of the issue, I think, is that routes are eagerly constructed with the `route_class` constructor in the `add_api_route` method, and then added to the `self.routes` attribute. Hence, overriding the route_class after the fact would perhaps require to reconstruct all the routes... I'm not sure that would be desireable nor possible... 

Still, I think the use case for 'dynamic route_class overriding' is there...

Please let me know if you think this makes sense for the project, and in that case whether you would like me to submit a more refined issue.

Thank you for this great project!

title: Alphabetically sort schemas
body:
Modify openapi spec generation to include schemas in alphabetical order.

-----

Right now, the schemas occur in a haphazard order in the generated OpenAPI spec (and therefore also at the bottom of the swagger docs), since it is generated based on the order in which schemas are encountered during the spec generation process.

This change would cause the definitions to occur in alphabetical order.
author: dmontagu

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @dmontagu ! :cake: :rocket: 

I haven't needed it, but I see how it could be useful. Thanks! :tada:

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I have found this useful for two things:

1. In the swagger docs, sometimes I just want to scroll down to the schema definition. It's almost impossible to find the right one when they are not alphabetically sorted! (Well, maybe I just have a lot of models, but it has been a frequent nuisance.)

2. I want my schema diffs to be as small as possible (so I can put them in version control and have it be useful for tracking changes). There are workarounds (like sorting the json keys), but they mostly have tradeoffs I don't like (the resulting schema is in a very unorthodox order).

Thanks for merging this!

title: Add better support for request body access/manipulation
body:
This pull request introduces a small change to `APIRoute` to make it easier to construct a custom `app` attribute in a custom subclass.

There is a lot of custom functionality that this change would simplify, but there are two especially important capabilities it adds:
* A way to get handle exceptions with access to the request body
* A way to modify the request body prior to its parsing/handling by FastAPI

I've added two sections to the "Using the Request Directly" docs showing how to use the changes in this PR to implement both of the above kinds of feature.

------

There have been many recent github issues that could be better-addressed with this capability.

**Related to accessing the body in middleware/exception handlers:**
* #180 accessing request body in middleware
* #375 accessing request body in exception handlers
* #394 accessing request body in middleware
* #575 accessing request body in exception handlers
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/493 accessing request body in exception handlers (using fastapi)
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495  a variety of use cases involving accessing request body in middleware (some using fastapi)

**Related to modifying the request prior to handling by FastAPI:**
* #521 support for msgpack-encoded requests
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/644 support for gzip-encoded requests (using fastapi)

-----
Quoting Tom Christie from https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495:

> "Consume request body in middleware is problematic"
>
> Indeed. Consuming request data in middleware is problematic.
> Not just to Starlette, but generally, everywhere.
>
> On the whole you should avoid doing so if at all possible.

I think using middleware to consume the request body is the obvious (or, perhaps, "naive") way to handle these issues. But that approach is explicitly discouraged, and not supported. So I think the approach described in / supported by this PR is especially valuable.

-----

(This pull request is in lieu of https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/568, and includes what I think is both the smallest and most important change to `APIRoute` from that pull request.)
author: dmontagu

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Awesome @dmontagu ! Thanks a lot! :tada: :rocket: :cake: 

I think this is sufficiently important to have its own section in the docs, so I moved it. I put it near the end as I consider this quite advanced and it could confuse newcomers.

I also added an extra example setting the `route_class` in a router. For that, I had to rebase from `master`, to include your recent fix :sweat_smile: 

Thanks again! :bowing_man: :taco:

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body:
Not sure if I'm describing things correctly in this block 😅, just wanted to flag that.

Also I'm not sure if the new `APIRoute.get_app` should be renamed. Since it just calls the `get_app` function that seemed like a good name to me though.

review_comments.author: tiangolo
review_comments.body:
Good point!

The name `get_app` is probably confusing for others.

The original name was based on how it was used in Starlette, which in turn was based on the ASGI spec (technically, a request handler is an ASGI app itself, that could stand by itself).

---

But it could clearly be confusing. I think we should rename it.

How about something like:

*  `request_function`
* `get_request_function`
* `request_handler`
* `get_request_handler`
* `get_handler`

What do you think?

Also, I think we should name the stand-alone function one way and the class method something different, to avoid the editor linking the function from inside the method to the method itself (instead of the function that lives outside).

review_comments.author: dmontagu
review_comments.body: I'm in favor of `get_request_handler` for the function and `get_route_handler` for the method. (As discussed on gitter.)

title: Dependencies with yield (used as context managers)
body:
Add support for context manager dependencies using functions with `yield`. Sync or async.

With inspiration from @dmontagu 's https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/570.

This handles dependencies that `yield` by converting them into a sync or async context manager,  depending on the function. And runs them in a "context manager stack".

This way, the order of dependencies is preserved and cleanup/closing happens in the correct order (the first dependency called is cleaned last).

This also means that dependencies that require other dependencies will still have them on scope during cleanup. So, contextmanager-style dependencies that require other contextmanager dependencies will have access to them during cleanup.

When a dependency function is a normal function (not `async`), the context manager is called in a threadpool (`__enter__`), so, the same as with normal dependencies, it's safe to do non-async blocking IO in contextmanager style dependencies. E.g. start a DB connection, probably the main use-case for this. And the same for cleaning it up (`__exit__`).

This works by default in Python 3.7.

And works in Python 3.6 if the backports are installed:

* `async-exit-stack`
* `async-generator`

```bash
pip install async-exit-stack async-generator
```

But they are not forced/required dependencies. If an app doesn't use context managers and still runs in Python 3.6, it will still work without the backports.

...Although I suspect most of the apps will use this for DB connections.

These backport dependencies are checked on app initialization, so, if a contextmanager-style dependency function (with `yield`s) is found, the environment is not Python 3.7+, and the backports are not installed, it will raise a `RuntimeError` right away on app initialization (so, no risk for the error lurking into a prod environment).

---

TODO:

* [x] Fix `mypy` types.
* [x] Extend tests to check for correct order of contextmanager dependencies with subdependencies.
* [x] Extend tests to verify that background tasks can still access the dependencies from context managers (e.g. DB connections).
* [x] Add docs.

title: Fix #259: generate OpenAPI docs for responses with no content.
body:
This is a backwards-compatible change, as far as I know, for responses that explicitly return content.  There are two basic changes:

* FastAPI now knows which response codes do not return bodies, and suppresses the `content` element in their API docs.
* If the response_class for a route has no media type, FastAPI assumes that it will not return content.  (If this route specifies additional responses that do return content, those responses are assumed to return `application/json` content and use an appropriate response class.)

This change introduces two new test files which check the new logic and keep the code coverage at 100%.

This change also updates the tutorial documentation to reflect the new functionality.

title: Fixing validator-caused incorrect output key order
body:
A quick PR that fixes #630. The cloned field was adding existing field validators before the fields themselves. 

This caused Python's default "ordered `dict`" behaviour to incorrectly output dictionary key/value pairs with all 'validated' fields first.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @dmontagu ! Awesome job! :clap: :clap: :clap: :cake: :rocket:

And thanks for the comments in the PR, they help a lot! :nerd_face: :memo: :1st_place_medal: 

---

I added some code to make it backwards compatible with Pydantic 0.32.2 with a lot of deprecation warnings. That should help developers migrating code bases.

Now all the tests still pass with Pydantic 0.32.2 except the tests for the updated OpenAPI schemas, as the schema titles now have spaces instead of underscores, and the strict booleans. But all the rest still passes. That means that developer's codebases that somehow require Pydantic 0.32.2 will still work as expected in the next version of FastAPI, although with deprecation warnings. 

I didn't add extra tests for that, and all the backwards compatible checks in FastAPI's codebase look kinda ugly and cumbersome, but I bet that might help some developers doing the migration more smoothly. Later, in a future version, I'll remove all that.

I tried extending the range to Pydantic v1.1.1, but as in FastAPI we are using some of the internals of Pydantic (things that wouldn't be covered by SemVer, so, it's not Pydantic's fault), some things have changed and break stuff here, so I think I'll just have to keep it pinned and update it often when there are new releases of Pydantic.

We can debug and fix what changed in v1.1.1 later, but that'll be in another PR, this one is good as it is right now.

I also updated the docs for the examples that changed.

---

This is ready to be merged now, I think everything's fine. And I see you dobule-triple-checked everything too :nerd_face: :100:

But as I added some extra stuff, and this is a big refactor, I wanted to give you the chance to check if you see anything that doesn't seem right, although it's not necessary. Otherwise, I'll just merge it tomorrow and release :rocket:

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@tiangolo for what it's worth, I found the two lines necessary to change for compatibility with pydantic v1.1.1 (it was surprisingly hard, given how small the fixes are):

* In fastapi.utils, line 148 needs to be changed from:
    ```python
    new_field._populate_validators()
    ```
    to
    ```python
    new_field.populate_validators()
    ```
    Looks like this became a public interface as of v1.1.1 (not v1.1); maybe @samuelcolvin can confirm. Note: @samuelcolvin it looks like this change isn't currently on the `master` branch of pydantic; not sure if that's intentional or not.

    **For FastAPI: Assuming the above analysis is right, this seems like a good candidate to address in a subsequent FastAPI release.**

* Immediately below line 375 of fastapi/dependencies/utils.py, you need to add the following line:
    ```python
    field.required = required
    ```
    It looks like this is a due to a regression from v1.0 to v1.1 where things annotated as `Any` became non-required. I believe this is a bug and will create an issue in pydantic.

    **For FastAPI: This seems like something that may be quickly addressed/clarified in pydantic, so presumably better to wait for a response there.**

After making these changes, all tests passed for me on v1.1.1. But I am in favor of holding off on a v1.1.1+-supporting release until we can get clarification on the points above.

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body:
I would strongly encourage fastapi to not constrain the version of pydantic beyond the major version. Doing so indicates that either pydantic is changing too much in minor versions or fastapi is using pydantic features which aren't supposed to be used externally - probably until both these are true.

To try and allow fast API to constrain pydantic stress strictly I would be happy to run all or most of the fastapi test suit during pydantic testing to avoid unexpected incompatibility. 

In addition fastapi should resist using private methods in pydantic, instead it would be better if you could submit a PR or issue to pydantic asking for a required feature to be made external, or implement the logic yourself.

Would you accept the approach of running fastapi's tests in return for not constraining pydantic beyond `>=1.1, <2` or similar?

---

Regarding the two points raised by @dmontagu above:
* `populate_validators` became public, probably best that you just require pydantic `>=v1.1` or maybe `>=1.2` since that will be released soon.
* I've answered the issue https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/1028 but let me know if there's anything else I can do to mitigate this?

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Awesome job debugging that @dmontagu ! :rocket: :nerd_face: 

> To try and allow fast API to constrain pydantic stress strictly I would be happy to run all or most of the fastapi test suit during pydantic testing to avoid unexpected incompatibility.

> Would you accept the approach of running fastapi's tests in return for not constraining pydantic beyond >=1.1, <2 or similar?

Oh, would you do that? I would love it!

> In addition fastapi should resist using private methods in pydantic, instead it would be better if you could submit a PR or issue to pydantic asking for a required feature to be made external, or implement the logic yourself.

That sounds great! I'll check for the next versions if I'm using something internal and request/PR to make it public.

In short, I would love to keep loose constraints on Pydantic versions.

---

I'll hold this PR, for now, to merge it after Pydantic v1.2 and relax the constraints.

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body:
Sounds good, let's do roughly the following:
* release pydantic v1.2
* fix and merge this PR 
* release the new version of fastapi
* get pydantic to run tests for the latest git tag of fastapi

comments.author: tiangolo

I just realized that if I'm adding all those temporary backward compatible layers (with deprecation warnings) to support Pydantic >= `0.32.2`, I might as well add the workaround / fix suggested by @dmontagu to also support Pydantic from `1.0.0` to `1.1.1`.

After https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/1031/ is done, everything will just keep working.

And later, when we remove support for Pydantic < `1.2.0`, we can remove those extras here.
